name: Orbital Research HKR Modular Payload Release System
description: Hunt-Kill-Return (HKR) modular payload release system for converting single-use FPV/UAS platforms into multi-mission lethal assets. Integrates with multiple UAS platforms per Army directed requirement. Supports interchangeable drop-glide munitions with anti-personnel and anti-armor warheads. Includes motion measurement package, common tail section, and modular fusing system. SBIR-funded, tested at Project Convergence Capstone 4 (Fort Irwin), AEWE 2023/2024/2025. $9M+ APFIT Phase III contract Sep 2022. 1,700 munitions and 24 UAVs procured for Soldier assessments.

compliance.note: US manufacturer (Cleveland, OH). SBIR Phase I/II/III. APFIT contract. SBIR CATALYST. Not commercially available — government procurement only via SBIR Phase III authority.

platform_compatibility: Multi-platform — adapted for multiple UAS per Army directed requirement for company-level sUAS supporting brigade combat teams

name: Orbital Research Drop-Glide Munition — Anti-Personnel
description: Low-cost drop-glide munition with interchangeable anti-personnel warhead for FPV/UAS delivery. Developed with Soldier feedback from Fort Benning (defeat ambush positions). Features guided and unguided variants, motion measurement package, and common tail section compatible with multiple warhead types. Released from multi-mission dropper kit. Live rounds tested on military ranges via Maneuver Battle Lab partnership. Part of HKR (Hunt-Kill-Return) system.

compliance.note: US manufacturer. Government procurement only. Not available commercially. Tested live at Maneuver Battle Lab ranges.

platform_compatibility: FPV drones and multi-rotor UAS with HKR modular release system installed

name: Orbital Research Drop-Glide Munition — Anti-Armor
description: Low-cost drop-glide munition with interchangeable anti-armor warhead for FPV/UAS delivery. Developed to defeat armored vehicles per Soldier feedback from Fort Benning. Shares common tail section and motion measurement package with anti-personnel variant. Supports real-time detection and geolocation of multiple enemy threats. Part of HKR (Hunt-Kill-Return) system. Tested live at Project Convergence Capstone 4, Fort Irwin and AEWE exercises.

compliance.note: US manufacturer. Government procurement only. Tested live at Project Convergence Capstone 4 and AEWE.

platform_compatibility: FPV drones and multi-rotor UAS with HKR modular release system installed

name: DEVCOM Audible Dropper — M67 Grenade Release System
description: 3D-printed munition dropper developed by the US Army Combat Capabilities Development Command (DEVCOM). Mounts under a Skydio X10D sUAS. Remotely pulls the M67 grenade pin mid-flight via electronic trigger, enabling precise delivery against static and moving targets. First live M67 grenade drop by conventional US Army forces conducted June 25, 2025 at Grafenwoehr Training Area, Germany (7ATC, 278th ACR, 173rd Airborne). Follow-on September 2025 trial dropped 117 live M67 grenades. Gen 2 version in development addressing release reliability and stabilization. Field-printable — soldiers can 3D print in theater using commercial CAD software and standard Army additive manufacturing kits. Part of Project Shiv experimentation lineage. Inspired by Ukraine FPV grenade-drop tactics.

compliance.note: US Army government-developed. Not commercially available. 3D-printable in field. No procurement pathway — DEVCOM internal.

programs: US Army Europe and Africa multi-domain modernization

name: Widowmaker 3D-Printed Grenade Dropper
description: Soldier-developed 3D-printed pylon-mounted munition dropper designed and fabricated entirely in theater by soldiers from the Multi Purpose Company, 1-502nd Infantry, 2nd Mobile BCT, 101st Airborne Division. Tested on a PDW C100 drone during Combined Resolve 26-1 at Grafenwoehr, October 9, 2025. Electronically triggered release mechanism. Demonstrated 4 grenades per sortie with accuracy from standoff ranges exceeding 100 meters. Compatible with M67 fragmentation grenades, M18 smoke grenades, and training munitions. Produced using commercial CAD software and standard Army additive manufacturing kits — no formal engineering background required. Represents squad-level organic air-delivered munitions capability. Pylon mounts under drone fuselage without compromising stability.
munition_compatibility: M67 fragmentation grenade, M18 smoke grenade, training munitions

compliance.note: Soldier-designed and fabricated in theater. Not commercially available. Open design — printable with standard Army AM kits.

name: UAV Systems International Payload Release Mechanism
description: Commercial servo-actuated payload release/drop mechanism for multi-rotor UAS. Supports payloads up to 5kg (11lb). Optimized for 3.5kg payloads. Triggered via remote controller button. Includes mounting hardware, setup, and controller configuration. Delivered in wooden box. NDAA 848 and TAA compliant. US-manufactured electronics (flight controllers and onboard electronics made in California). Used in NATO ammo resupply trial with Aurelia X6 Pro fleet — soldiers activated drop via helmet-mounted button to GPS coordinates. Also used for medical supply delivery in Sierra Leone and photogrammetry surveys in Canada. Available for Tarot X6, Tarot T-18, Tarot 650, and universal configurations.
compatible_platform: Tarot X6, Tarot T-18, Tarot 650, Aurelia X6 Pro, universal mounting

compliance.note: NDAA 848 and TAA compliant. US electronics (California). Commercially available. NATO trial use documented.

programs: NATO ammo resupply trial (Aurelia X6 Pro fleet)
